name: Yellow Oleander
description: Yellow Oleander (Cascabela thevetia, formerly Thevetia peruviana) is closely related to common Oleander and equally dangerous. All parts — seeds, leaves, bark, flowers, and fruit — contain thevetin A and thevetin B, potent cardiac glycosides related to digitalis. Seeds are most concentrated and are responsible for the majority of reported poisonings.
safetyNotes: Toxic compounds: thevetin A and thevetin B (cardiac glycosides) in all parts; seeds most concentrated
Mechanism: thevetins inhibit Na+/K+-ATPase in cardiac muscle, disrupting electrical conduction causing arrhythmias and heart block
Symptoms: vomiting, diarrhea, drooling, severe weakness, bradycardia, cardiac arrhythmias, hypotension, collapse; often fatal without treatment
Yellow Oleander seeds are particularly accessible when the fruit falls; the decorative yellow flowers are also attractive
Any suspected ingestion is a veterinary emergency

symptoms.name: Vomiting, diarrhea, excessive drooling, and abdominal pain
symptoms.onset: Within the first 2 hours after exposure
symptoms.notes: Gastrointestinal distress usually acts as the initial sign of poisoning from the plant's cardiac glycosides, often appearing just prior to major heart function abnormalities34. Symptoms can persist for up to 4 to 5 days3. 2. Abnormal Heart Rate and Arrhythmias

symptoms.name: Bradycardia (abnormally slow heart rate) or tachycardia (rapid heart rate), and severe arrhythmias
symptoms.onset: Expected within the first 2 hours after exposure
symptoms.notes: Cardiac glycosides directly interfere with the heart by inhibiting the Na + /K + -ATPase pump, altering intracellular calcium and sodium levels [645, Conversation History]. This causes the heart's contractions to become forceful but irregular, which can rapidly lead to life-threatening cardiovascula

symptoms.name: Hyperkalemia (elevated potassium levels in the blood)
symptoms.onset: Within the first 2 hours after exposure
symptoms.notes: This dangerous accumulation of extracellular potassium is a direct result of the disrupted cellular pumps35. Severe hyperkalemia (> 7.5–9mEq/L) requires aggressive, emergency intravenous intervention (such as sodium bicarbonate or dextrose/insulin therapy) to stabilize the cat3. 4. Weakness and Leth

symptoms.name: Weakness and lethargy
symptoms.onset: Within the first 2 hours after exposure
symptoms.notes: The profound weakness is a systemic reflection of the severe cardiovascular disruption and plummeting blood pressure34. 5. Tremors and Seizures

symptoms.name: Tremors and seizures
symptoms.onset: Often within the first few hours, progressing as the toxicosis worsens
symptoms.notes: These severe neurological manifestations occur in advanced stages of cardiac glycoside poisoning from oleander species26. 6. Difficulty Breathing

symptoms.name: Respiratory changes and difficulty breathing
symptoms.onset: Usually presents as the toxicity progresses (within hours)
symptoms.notes: Respiratory distress typically accompanies the severe, systemic decline in cardiovascular output and neurological stability
